questions.q: A Galois representation of $G_\mathbb{Q}$ is a:
questions.hint: By definition $\rho$ is a continuous homomorphism from the profinite group $G_\mathbb{Q}$ to a linear group; continuity against the target's topology (complex, $\ell$-adic, or mod-$\ell$) is what…
questions.explain: By definition $\rho$ is a continuous homomorphism from the profinite group $G_\mathbb{Q}$ to a linear group; continuity against the target's topology (complex, $\ell$-adic, or mod-$\ell$) is what distinguishes Artin, $\ell$-adic, and residual representations.

questions.q: Which statement about Artin representations (coefficients in $\mathbb{C}$) is correct?
choices: they factor through $\mathrm{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})$ for some finite Galois $L/\mathbb{Q}$
questions.hint: Continuity $G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the discrete image topology (any compact subgroup of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is a real Lie group with no small profinite subgroups)…
questions.explain: Continuity $G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the discrete image topology (any compact subgroup of $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is a real Lie group with no small profinite subgroups) forces finite image, i.e. factorisation through some finite $\mathrm{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})$.

questions.q: For the $\ell$-adic cyclotomic character $\chi:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathbb{Z}_\ell^\times$, what is $\chi(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ when $p\ne\ell$ is unramified? (Take $p=7$.)
questions.explain: On $\mu_{\ell^n}$, $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ acts by $\zeta\mapsto\zeta^p$, so $\chi(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=p$. For $p=7$ the value is $7$.

hard.q: Let $\rho:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_2(\bar{\mathbb{Q}}_\ell)$ be an $\ell$-adic representation and $\sigma\in G_\mathbb{Q}$ an element of infinite order. Which property is NOT guaranteed to hold for $\rho(\sigma)$?
choices: $\rho$ evaluated on the topological closure $\overline{\langle\sigma\rangle}$ is continuous
hard.hint: Continuity forces $\rho$ to factor through a finite quotient in the complex case only via compactness.
hard.explain: For Artin (complex) representations continuity forces finite image, but $\ell$-adic representations can have infinite image — e.g., the cyclotomic character. An element $\sigma$ of infinite order in $G_\mathbb{Q}$ need not map to a matrix of finite order.

hard.q: Consider the proof that every continuous $\rho:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ has finite image. Which step is the critical one?
choices: Any Hausdorff quotient of a profinite group is profinite, and $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ contains no infinite profinite subgroup because its subgroups that are both compact and totally disconnected are finite
The image is a closed subgroup of a compact Lie group, hence finite by dimension counting
$G_\mathbb{Q}$ is finite, so any homomorphic image is finite
All representations of Galois groups have finite image by the Langlands program
hard.hint: Compact image plus discreteness of $GL_n(\mathbb{C})$ subgroup structure restricts the image.
hard.explain: The key step: $G_\mathbb{Q}$ is profinite (compact, totally disconnected), so any continuous image in $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ must be compact and totally disconnected. But $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{C})$ is a real Lie group; its compact totally disconnected subgroups are finite (they have no small subgroups by a theorem on Lie groups).

hard.q: The mod-$\ell$ cyclotomic character $\bar\chi_\ell:G_\mathbb{Q}\to(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\mathbb{Z})^\times$ satisfies $\bar\chi_\ell(\mathrm{Frob}_p)\equiv p\pmod{\ell}$ for $p\ne\ell$. For $\ell=5$ and $p=11$, what is $\bar\chi_5(\mathrm{Frob}_{11})$?
hard.hint: The cyclotomic character acts by $\ell$-th power on roots of unity.

questions.q: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with good reduction at $p$, the characteristic polynomial of $\rho_{E,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ on the Tate module is:
questions.hint: The determinant is the cyclotomic character, so $\det=p$, and the trace is the Frobenius trace $a_p$: $\det(tI-\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p))=t^2-a_p t+p$.
questions.explain: The determinant is the cyclotomic character, so $\det=p$, and the trace is the Frobenius trace $a_p$: $\det(tI-\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p))=t^2-a_p t+p$.

questions.q: An elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ has good reduction at $p=11$ and $|\tilde E(\mathbb{F}_{11})|=10$. Compute $a_{11}=p+1-|\tilde E(\mathbb{F}_p)|$.
questions.explain: $a_{11}=11+1-10=2$. This is the trace of $\mathrm{Frob}_{11}$ on $V_\ell E$.

questions.q: By Hasse, $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$. What is the largest integer $a_p$ can be for $p=37$?
questions.hint: $2\sqrt{37}\approx 12.166$, so the largest integer value of $a_p$ is $12$.
questions.explain: $2\sqrt{37}\approx 12.166$, so the largest integer value of $a_p$ is $12$.

hard.q: The Weil conjectures (proved by Deligne) state that for a smooth projective variety over $\mathbb{F}_q$, the eigenvalues of Frobenius on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ have absolute value $q^{i/2}$. For an elliptic curve this gives $|\alpha|=|\beta|=\sqrt{p}$ where $t^2-a_p t+p=(t-\alpha)(t-\beta)$. Which of the following is a valid chain of implications?
choices: Hasse bound implies the eigenvalues are real
hard.hint: Weil II shows Frobenius eigenvalues are algebraic integers of bounded archimedean size.
hard.explain: The Weil conjectures give $|\alpha|=|\beta|=\sqrt{p}$; the triangle inequality then recovers Hasse's $|a_p|\le 2\sqrt{p}$. This is the correct logical chain: Weil conjectures are the deep statement, and the Hasse bound follows as a corollary.

hard.q: For the elliptic curve $E:y^2=x^3-x$ over $\mathbb{F}_5$, the points are $(0,0),(1,0),(4,0),(2,1),(2,4),(3,2),(3,3)$ plus the point at infinity, giving $|E(\mathbb{F}_5)|=8$. Compute $a_5$.

Two $\ell$-adic representations $\rho_1,\rho_2:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_n(\bar{\mathbb{Q}}_\ell)$ that are both unramified outside a finite set $S$ and satisfy $\operatorname{tr}\rho_1(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=\operatorname{tr}\rho_2(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$ for all $p\notin S$ are related by the Chebotarev density theorem as follows:
choices: They differ by a twist by the cyclotomic character
hard.hint: Chebotarev density plus traces match uniquely when semisimplifications agree.
hard.explain: Chebotarev implies Frobenius elements are dense, so equal traces at almost all primes forces equal characteristic polynomials for all $g\in G_\mathbb{Q}$. By Brauer–Nesbitt, two semisimple representations with the same character are isomorphic; hence $\rho_1^{\mathrm{ss}}\cong\rho_2^{\mathrm{ss}}$.

questions.q: Let $\rho:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_2(\bar{\mathbb{Q}}_\ell)$ be reducible but not semisimple, with an invariant line on which $G_\mathbb{Q}$ acts by $\chi_1$ and quotient $\chi_2$. What is $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$?
questions.hint: Semisimplification replaces a Jordan–Hölder filtration by the direct sum of its graded pieces.
questions.explain: Semisimplification replaces a Jordan–Hölder filtration by the direct sum of its graded pieces. The graded pieces here are $\chi_1$ and $\chi_2$, so $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}=\chi_1\oplus\chi_2$.

questions.q: Why do Frobenius traces fail to distinguish $\rho$ from $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$?
choices: because $\rho$ and $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$ have the same determinant
because trace is additive on short exact sequences, so $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=\operatorname{tr}\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)$
questions.hint: For any filtration $0\subset V_1\subset V$ by $G$-stable subspaces, $\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V)=\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V_1)+\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V/V_1)=\operatorname{tr}(g\mid\mathrm{gr}\,V)$.
questions.explain: For any filtration $0\subset V_1\subset V$ by $G$-stable subspaces, $\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V)=\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V_1)+\operatorname{tr}(g\mid V/V_1)=\operatorname{tr}(g\mid\mathrm{gr}\,V)$. So the trace only depends on $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$.

questions.q: Suppose $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}=\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$ where $\chi$ is the $\ell$-adic cyclotomic character. Compute $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_5)$ at an unramified prime $p=5$.

hard.q: Let $\rho=\begin{pmatrix}\chi & *\\ 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}$ be a non-split extension of $\mathbf{1}$ by $\chi$ (cyclotomic). Which statement correctly identifies $\rho$ vs $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$ and their Frobenius traces?
choices: $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}=\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$; $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=p+1$ for unramified $p$; yet $\rho\not\cong\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$ since the extension class is nontrivial
$\rho=\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$ because all reducible representations are semisimple
hard.hint: Non-split extensions and split ones share the same semisimplification.
hard.explain: Semisimplification of a non-split extension $0\to\chi\to\rho\to\mathbf{1}\to 0$ is $\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$. Frobenius trace equals $\chi(\mathrm{Frob}_p)+1=p+1$ for unramified $p$. The extension itself is nontrivial (e.g., arises in the $\ell$-adic rep associated to a CM elliptic curve at supersingular primes), so $\rho\ncong\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}$.

hard.q: Which is the correct counterexample showing that $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}\cong\sigma^{\mathrm{ss}}$ does NOT imply $\rho\cong\sigma$?
choices: A non-split extension $0\to\chi\to\rho\to\mathbf{1}\to 0$ and the split $\sigma=\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$ both have $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}\cong\sigma^{\mathrm{ss}}\cong\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$, yet $\rho\not\cong\sigma$ when the extension class is nontrivial in $\operatorname{Ext}^1_{G}(\mathbf{1},\chi)$
Two irreducible representations with the same dimension but different traces
The zero representation and any nontrivial representation
Any two representations of the same dimension are semisimplification-equivalent
hard.hint: Find a reducible representation whose semisimplification equals an indecomposable one.
hard.explain: Semisimplification loses information about extension classes. A non-split extension and the direct sum share the same semisimplification; this is the canonical counterexample. Extensions are classified by $H^1(G_\mathbb{Q}, \chi\otimes\mathbf{1}^\vee)=H^1(G_\mathbb{Q},\chi)$, which can be nontrivial.

hard.q: Let $\rho^{\mathrm{ss}}=\chi^2\oplus\chi\oplus\mathbf{1}$ where $\chi$ is the $\ell$-adic cyclotomic character. Compute $\operatorname{tr}\rho(\mathrm{Frob}_3)$ for the unramified prime $p=3$.
hard.hint: The determinant multiplies character weights; here $2+1+0=3$.

questions.q: A Galois representation $\rho:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_n(K)$ is unramified at a prime $p$ when:
choices: the inertia subgroup $I_p$ is in the kernel of $\rho$
questions.hint: Unramified at $p$ means $\rho(I_p)=1$; only then does $\rho$ factor through $D_p/I_p$ and $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ gets a well-defined image.
questions.explain: Unramified at $p$ means $\rho(I_p)=1$; only then does $\rho$ factor through $D_p/I_p$ and $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ gets a well-defined image.

questions.q: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with multiplicative reduction at $p$, the conductor exponent $f_p$ of $\rho_{E,\ell}$ is:
questions.hint: Multiplicative reduction is tame: inertia has a 1-dimensional invariant subspace on $V_\ell E$, so $f_p=\dim V-\dim V^{I_p}=2-1=1$ and the wild term vanishes.
questions.explain: Multiplicative reduction is tame: inertia has a 1-dimensional invariant subspace on $V_\ell E$, so $f_p=\dim V-\dim V^{I_p}=2-1=1$ and the wild term vanishes.

questions.q: A representation $\rho$ has $\dim V=3$, $\dim V^{I_p}=2$, and trivial wild ramification at $p$. What is the Artin conductor exponent $f_p(\rho)$?
questions.hint: With trivial wild term, $f_p=\dim V-\dim V^{I_p}=3-2=1$.
questions.explain: With trivial wild term, $f_p=\dim V-\dim V^{I_p}=3-2=1$.

hard.q: For an elliptic curve $E/\mathbb{Q}$ with additive reduction at $p$, which statement about the conductor exponent $f_p$ is correct?
choices: $f_p=1$ since inertia acts on a codimension-1 subspace
$f_p\ge 2$ since $\dim V^{I_p}=0$ and the wild part $\delta_p\ge 0$ contributes
hard.hint: Additive reduction forces conductor exponent at least $2$.
hard.explain: Additive reduction means $V_\ell E$ has no inertia invariants ($\dim V^{I_p}=0$), so the tame part contributes $\dim V-\dim V^{I_p}=2$. The wild part $\delta_p\ge 0$ (it vanishes only for $p\ge 5$). Thus $f_p=2+\delta_p\ge 2$.

hard.q: A 4-dimensional representation $\rho$ has $\dim V^{I_p}=1$ and wild conductor exponent $\delta_p=3$. Compute $f_p(\rho)$.
Place the following steps in the correct logical order for the proof that the global conductor $N=\prod_p p^{f_p}$ is well-defined and finite for a representation $\rho$ unramified outside $S$:
choices: (1) For $p\notin S$, $I_p\subset\ker\rho$ so $f_p=0$; (2) For $p\in S$, $f_p$ is finite since $V$ is finite-dimensional and higher ramification groups act trivially for large enough index; (3) $N=\prod_{p\in S}p^{f_p}$ is a finite product of primes each to a finite power
(1) $N$ is defined as the level of the associated modular form; (2) Finiteness follows from modularity
(1) Each $f_p$ is infinite; (2) the product is still finite
(1) Wild ramification is always trivial; (2) $N=\prod p^{\dim V - \dim V^{I_p}}$
hard.hint: Well-definedness needs local-global compatibility across places.
hard.explain: The key steps: $f_p=0$ for unramified primes by definition; for ramified primes the filtration of higher ramification groups eventually stabilizes (acting trivially) since $\rho$ factors through a finite extension of the completion, making $f_p$ finite; the conductor $N$ is then a well-defined positive integer.

questions.q: Deligne attaches to a weight-$k$ cuspidal eigenform $f$ with character $\varepsilon$ a 2-dim $\ell$-adic $\rho_{f,\lambda}$ satisfying, for $p\nmid N\ell$:
questions.hint: Eichler–Shimura/Deligne: Fourier coefficients at unramified primes are traces of Frobenius, and the determinant is $\varepsilon\cdot\chi^{k-1}$ (the cyclotomic character raised to the $k-1$ power,…
questions.explain: Eichler–Shimura/Deligne: Fourier coefficients at unramified primes are traces of Frobenius, and the determinant is $\varepsilon\cdot\chi^{k-1}$ (the cyclotomic character raised to the $k-1$ power, times the nebentypus).

questions.q: A residual representation $\bar\rho:G_\mathbb{Q}\to\mathrm{GL}_2(\bar{\mathbb{F}}_\ell)$ is called modular if:
choices: it arises as the reduction mod $\ell$ of some $\rho_{f,\lambda}$ for a cuspidal eigenform $f$
questions.hint: Modularity of a residual (mod-$\ell$) representation means it comes from a cuspidal eigenform $f$ via $\bar\rho\cong\bar\rho_{f,\lambda}$.
questions.explain: Modularity of a residual (mod-$\ell$) representation means it comes from a cuspidal eigenform $f$ via $\bar\rho\cong\bar\rho_{f,\lambda}$. Modularity lifting theorems ($R=T$) propagate modularity from $\bar\rho$ to its $\ell$-adic lifts.

questions.q: The weight-12 cusp form $\Delta=\sum\tau(n)q^n$ has $\tau(2)=-24$. By Deligne, what is $\operatorname{tr}\rho_{\Delta,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_2)$ for $\ell\ne 2$?
questions.hint: Deligne's construction gives $\operatorname{tr}\rho_{\Delta,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=\tau(p)$, so the trace at $p=2$ is $\tau(2)=-24$.
questions.explain: Deligne's construction gives $\operatorname{tr}\rho_{\Delta,\ell}(\mathrm{Frob}_p)=\tau(p)$, so the trace at $p=2$ is $\tau(2)=-24$.

hard.q: In the $R=T$ modularity lifting theorem, what role does the condition that $\bar\rho$ is absolutely irreducible play?
choices: It ensures $R$ and $T$ are both zero, trivializing the problem
It guarantees that the universal deformation ring $R$ is well-defined and that the map $R\to T$ can be shown to be an isomorphism via Taylor–Wiles patching
It makes $\bar\rho$ modular automatically without any lifting
It forces all deformations of $\bar\rho$ to have finite image
hard.hint: Absolute irreducibility eliminates extra deformations from reducible subreps.
hard.explain: Absolute irreducibility of $\bar\rho$ is needed for Mazur's deformation theory: it ensures the functor of lifts is representable by a pro-ring $R$. Without it, deformations may not be classified by a single universal object. The $R=T$ theorem then uses Taylor–Wiles patching to show $R\cong T$, lifting modularity from $\bar\rho$ to the $\ell$-adic representation.

hard.q: For $f=\Delta$ of weight 12, the Weil conjectures give $|\alpha|=|\beta|=p^{(k-1)/2}$ for eigenvalues of $\mathrm{Frob}_p$ on $V_\ell\Delta$. Compute $|\alpha|$ at $p=4$ (i.e., $p=2^2$ — but use $p=2$, $k=12$): $2^{(12-1)/2}=2^{5.5}$. To the nearest integer, what is $\lfloor 2^{5.5}\rfloor$?
hard.hint: Use the Ramanujan bound $|a_p| \le 2 p^{11/2}$ for weight $12$.

hard.q: Which sequence of steps correctly outlines the proof of Fermat's Last Theorem via Galois representations?
choices: (1) Assume $a^p+b^p=c^p$; (2) Form the Frey curve $E:y^2=x(x-a^p)(x+b^p)$; (3) Ribet's level-lowering shows $\bar\rho_{E,p}$ would have to come from a weight-2 form of level 2; (4) No such form exists; (5) Contradiction
(1) Apply the Hasse bound to get $a_p=0$; (2) Conclude $E$ has no rational points
(1) Use class field theory to show $\mathbb{Q}$ has no extensions of degree $p$; (2) Fermat follows
(1) Assume $a^p+b^p=c^p$; (2) Show $E$ has good reduction everywhere; (3) Modularity gives a contradiction
hard.hint: Frey curve, modularity, level-lowering, then Ribet plus Wiles.
hard.explain: The Wiles–Taylor proof: (1) the Frey curve is semistable; (2) Serre's conjecture (Ribet's theorem) implies $\bar\rho_{E,p}$ descends to level 2; (3) there are no cuspidal eigenforms of weight 2 and level 2; (4) Wiles' modularity lifting gives $\rho_{E,p}$ is modular, so $\bar\rho_{E,p}$ modular — contradiction.
